title: Framebuffers & Multi-Pass Rendering
description: Real post-processing pipelines work by rendering a scene to an offscreen framebuffer object (FBO), then reading that texture in a second pass to apply blur, bloom, or tone mapping. This renderer runs single-pass only, so the challenges below approximate multi-pass effects using neighbor sampling within one shader — giving you the intuition for what a real FBO pipeline does under the hood.

theory.fbo_concept: A framebuffer object (FBO) is an offscreen render target: instead of writing to the screen, the GPU writes pixel colors into a texture. A subsequent draw call samples that texture as input, enabling effects that require reading neighboring pixels from a completed previous frame.
theory.why_two_pass: A separable Gaussian blur needs two passes: one horizontal, one vertical. Each pass reads from the previous output. Doing both in a single pass would require a 2D kernel (NxN samples) and produce slightly incorrect results — true multi-pass is the correct approach in production.
theory.single_pass_approximation: In a single pass, you can approximate blur by sampling the current input texture at several UV offsets and averaging the results. This is a box blur approximation — correct if the input is already fully rendered, which in a real pipeline means sampling from an FBO color attachment.
theory.bloom: Bloom is typically: (1) render scene to FBO, (2) threshold bright areas into a second FBO, (3) blur the threshold buffer, (4) add it back to the original. In a single pass on a static texture you can simulate it with a smoothstep glow around bright regions.

challenges.title: Box Blur via Neighbor Sampling
challenges.description: Simulate a blur pass by sampling the checkerboard texture at the current UV plus 8 neighboring offsets (a 3×3 box kernel minus the center), averaging all 9 samples. This mimics reading from a completed previous render, as a real multi-pass blur would do.
hints: Inside the loop, sample `texture(u_texture0, uv + vec2(float(x), float(y)) * texel)`. `texel` is one pixel in UV space — `1.0 / u_resolution`. Multiply by a blur radius (3.0) to widen the effect.

After the nested loops, divide `col` by 9.0 (the number of samples in a 3×3 grid) to get the average. In a real multi-pass pipeline, `u_texture0` would be replaced by the FBO color attachment from the previous pass.
challenges.unlocks_theory: This 3×3 box filter is functionally identical to what a first blur pass does in a real FBO pipeline — the only difference is that in a true multi-pass setup, the input texture contains a completed rendered image rather than a static asset. The separability trick (one horizontal + one vertical pass) reduces O(N²) samples to O(2N) and is how real-time Gaussian blur achieves its performance.

hlsl.note: HLSL loop variables must be explicitly cast to float for arithmetic.

challenges.title: Fake Bloom with Smoothstep Glow
challenges.description: Create a glowing bright spot in the center of the canvas using a single-pass bloom approximation. Use the radial distance from the center to define a brightness falloff, then add a `smoothstep`-based glow halo around a bright core. This mimics what a bloom pass adds on top of a rendered scene.
hints: Core: `1.0 - smoothstep(0.0, 0.08, dist)` creates a tight white circle of radius ~0.08.
Bloom halo: `(1.0 - smoothstep(0.0, 0.6, dist)) * 0.4` is a large, dim radial falloff. Adding it to the core creates the soft glow you'd see from a real bloom post-process pass.
challenges.unlocks_theory: This single-pass approximation captures the essential look of bloom but lacks the frequency separation of a true multi-pass pipeline. Real bloom thresholds pixels above a luminance value, blurs only those bright regions in a separate buffer, then additively composites the result. The additive blend — core + bloom — is unchanged between both approaches.

challenges.title: Chromatic Aberration
challenges.description: Simulate chromatic aberration — a lens artifact where different wavelengths focus at slightly different points — by sampling the red, green, and blue channels at slightly offset UVs. This is a classic single-pass post-process effect that would normally read from an FBO.
hints: The red channel is sampled at `uv + dir * strength` — pushed outward from center. Blue is pushed in the opposite direction: `uv - dir * strength`. Green stays at the original UV.

`dir = uv - 0.5` gives a vector pointing outward from the center of the canvas. Scaling by `strength` (0.01–0.03) keeps the effect subtle. On a real post-process pipeline, this reads from the full rendered frame in an FBO.
challenges.unlocks_theory: Chromatic aberration is a staple post-process effect in modern game engines. In a real FBO pipeline each channel sample draws from the same frame texture — the aberration emerges because each RGB sample reads from a slightly different location in that completed frame. The math you wrote here is identical to production implementations.
